sanskrit_iast:
naiva kiñcit karomīti yukto manyeta tattva-vit |
paśyañ śṛṇvan spṛśañ jighrann aśnan gacchan svapan śvasan || 8 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: A person in the divine consciousness, although engaged in seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, moving about, sleeping and breathing, always knows within himself that he actually does nothing at all. Because while speaking, evacuating, receiving, or opening or closing his eyes, he always knows that only the material senses are engaged with their objects and that he is aloof from them.

A person in the divine consciousness, although engaged in seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, moving about, sleeping and breathing, always knows within himself that he actually does nothing at all. Because while speaking, evacuating, receiving, or opening or closing his eyes, he always knows that only the material senses are engaged with their objects and that he is aloof from them.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1093 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A person in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is pure in his existence, and consequently he has nothing to do with any work which depends upon five immediate and remote causes: the doer, the work, the situation, the endeavor and fortune. This is because he is engaged in the loving transcendental service of Kṛṣṇa.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.8 Yuktah, remaining absorbed in the Self; tattva-vit, the knower of Reality-knower of the real nature of Truth, of the Self, i.e., the seer of the supreme Reality; manyeta, should think; ‘na karomi eva, I certainly do not do; kincit, anything.’ Having realized the Truth, when or how should he thin…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the tattva-vit (knower of truth), absorbed in the Self, should think 'I do nothing at all' — even while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, going, sleeping, and breathing. Shankara emphasises this is the realised non-doership of the established jñānin: the senses operate, the body acts, but the Self does not act. This is the cessation of the false agency-attribution to the Self.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.8 – 5.9 Thus he who knows the truth concerning the self should reflect in mind that the ear and the other organs of sensation (Jnanendriyas) as also organs of action (Karmendriyas) and the vital currents (the Pranas) are occupied with their own respective objects.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja groups 5.8 and 5.9 together: the one who knows the truth concerning the self should reflect that the jñānendriyas, karmendriyas, and the prāṇas are occupied with their own respective objects. He should know 'I do not do anything at all' — meaning, the self (which is distinct from these instruments) does not do; the instruments do. Ramanuja's reading is dualistic: the self is the experiencer/witness, the body-mind-prāṇa complex is the doer.

positions.rendering: always knows within himself that he actually does nothing at all (devotional consciousness)
Advaita: the Self does not act; the body-senses act; the jñānin recognises this and disowns false agency
Vishishtadvaita: the self is distinct from body-mind-prāṇa instruments; the instruments do, the self does not

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Major doctrinal divergence. Shankara: agency-of-the-Self is illusory; only senses-and-body act. Ramanuja: agency-of-the-jīva is real but pertains to body-mind-prāṇa, not to the self proper. Prabhupada renders devotionally. All three preserve the structural claim: 'I' (in the proper sense) do nothing while seeing/hearing/etc.

literal_meaning: The yogi knower-of-truth should think 'I do nothing at all' — while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, going, sleeping, and breathing.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the doctrine of non-doership for the seer-of-truth. Across the most basic activities of embodied life — perception, locomotion, alimentation, respiration — the tattva-vit recognises that the senses and body are operating; the Self does not. This is structural-perceptual, not vocational. The verse continues into 5.9 and is read as a pair with it.

remaining_gaps: vedabase.io and bhagavad-gita.us both render 5.8 and 5.9 on combined pages (5/8-9 and 5/8 respectively); the same verbatim translation/commentary covers both verses. holy-bhagavad-gita.org and gitasupersite.iitk.ac.in render them separately. The combined-page rendering is recorded honestly here.
